CSI.description: The ESC [ prefix that starts most terminal escape sequences. Defined in ECMA-48.

SGR.description: Escape sequences for text styling: bold, italic, underline, colors, etc.

OSC.description: Escape sequences for application-to-terminal communication: window title, clipboard, hyperlinks.

DCS.description: Escape sequences for device-specific data (Sixel graphics, DECRQSS queries).

DEC.description: The company that created the VT100 terminal series. Their private mode sequences (DECSET/DECRST) are still the primary mechanism for terminal feature negotiation.

ECMA-48.description: The foundational standard for terminal control sequences, first published in 1976. Also known as ISO 6429 and (formerly) ANSI X3.64.

ANSI.description: Published the X3.64 terminal standard in 1979, withdrawn in 1994 in favor of ISO 6429. The name persists in 'ANSI escape codes'.

DECRPM.description: Response sequence reporting whether a DEC private mode is set, reset, or unrecognized.

DECRQSS.description: Query the terminal for current settings (SGR attributes, scroll region, etc.).

DECSET.description: CSI ? Pm h — enable a DEC private mode (alt screen, mouse tracking, bracketed paste, etc.).

DECRST.description: CSI ? Pm l — disable a DEC private mode.

DECTCEM.description: DEC private mode ?25 — show/hide the text cursor.

DECAWM.description: DEC private mode ?7 — automatically wrap text at the right margin.

DECSTBM.expansion: DEC Set Top and Bottom Margins
DECSTBM.description: Define the scroll region — lines between top and bottom margins scroll independently.

DECSC.description: Save cursor position and attributes for later restoration with DECRC.

DECSCUSR.description: Set cursor shape: block, underline, or bar; blinking or steady.

DA1.description: Query the terminal for its type and supported features. The terminal responds with its capability flags.

DSR.description: Query the terminal for status information, most commonly cursor position (DSR 6).

CUP.description: CSI row ; col H — move cursor to absolute position. The most fundamental cursor command.

EL.description: Clear part of the current line (right of cursor, left of cursor, or entire line).

ED.description: Clear part of the screen (below cursor, above cursor, or entire screen).

ICH.description: Insert blank characters at cursor position, shifting existing characters right.

DCH.description: Delete characters at cursor position, shifting remaining characters left.

RIS.description: Full terminal reset — cursor, modes, scroll regions, character sets, colors all reset.

VT100.description: DEC's 1978 terminal that defined the escape sequence grammar used by every modern terminal emulator.

VT220.description: DEC's 1983 terminal that added insert/delete operations (ICH, DCH, IL, DL) and 8-bit controls.

VT52.description: DEC's 1975 pre-ANSI terminal with proprietary escape sequences predating ECMA-48. First DEC video terminal with cursor addressing.

VT510.description: One of DEC's later VT terminals (1993). Added cursor visibility (DECTCEM), reverse video (DECSCNM), and scroll commands (SU/SD).

xterm.description: The reference X11 terminal emulator (1984), maintained by Thomas Dickey since 1996. Defined most 'standard' terminal behavior including 256-color, truecolor, mouse tracking, and bracketed paste.

TEA.description: A functional UI pattern: Model → Update → View. Used by Bubbletea (Go) and Silvery's optional app framework.

TUI.description: A graphical user interface rendered in a terminal using text characters and escape sequences.

PTY.description: A virtual terminal device that allows a process to interact with a terminal emulator as if it were a real serial terminal.

XTGETTCAP.description: Query the terminal for a specific terminfo capability value via DCS.

terminal emulator.description: A program that simulates a hardware terminal. Ghostty, iTerm2, and Windows Terminal are terminal emulators — they display text and interpret escape sequences.

shell.description: The command interpreter (bash, zsh, fish) that runs inside a terminal emulator. The shell handles commands; the terminal handles display.

escape sequence.description: A special byte pattern starting with ESC (0x1B) that tells the terminal to do something other than display text — like change colors, move the cursor, or switch modes.

scrollback.description: The history of text that has scrolled off the top of the terminal screen. You scroll up to see it. Size varies by terminal (typically 1,000–100,000 lines).

alternate screen.description: A separate screen that full-screen apps (vim, less, htop) switch to. When the app exits, your previous terminal content reappears. An xterm invention, not part of any formal standard.

bracketed paste.description: A safety feature: the terminal wraps pasted text in special markers so the shell knows it's pasted, not typed. Prevents accidental command execution from pasting text containing newlines.

truecolor.description: Specify any of 16.7 million colors using red/green/blue values (0-255 each). Before truecolor, terminals were limited to 256 indexed colors.

mouse tracking.description: Terminal reports mouse clicks, movement, and scroll wheel events to the application.

synchronized output.description: Buffer all output between start/end markers and paint as one frame. Eliminates flicker.

text reflow.description: When you resize the terminal window, wrapped lines re-wrap to fit the new width instead of staying broken. Not all terminals support this.

wide character.description: A character that occupies two terminal columns instead of one. CJK ideographs (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) and many emoji are wide characters.

CJK.description: The three East Asian writing systems whose characters are 'fullwidth' — they occupy two terminal columns each. Correct width calculation is essential for TUI layout.

grapheme cluster.description: What humans perceive as a single character — which may be multiple Unicode codepoints. The family emoji is 7 codepoints but one grapheme cluster occupying 2 terminal columns.

headless.description: A terminal emulator library running without a visible window — used for automated testing. It parses escape sequences but doesn't render pixels.

reverse index.description: Move the cursor up one line. If already at the top of the scroll region, scroll the content down instead. The opposite of a newline.

auto-wrap.description: When text reaches the right edge of the terminal, automatically continue on the next line. Almost always enabled — disabling it causes text to overwrite the last column.

semantic prompts.description: Invisible markers that tell the terminal where each shell prompt, command, and output starts and ends. Enables click-to-navigate between commands and red/green indicators for success/failure.

RGB.description: Color model using three values (0-255 each) to specify any of 16.7 million colors. Used by truecolor escape sequences.

terminfo.description: A compiled database mapping terminal names to their capabilities (colors, cursor control, key sequences). Used by ncurses and most Unix TUI libraries. Limited to capabilities known at compile time — cannot describe modern features like Kitty keyboard or OSC 8.

termcap.description: The predecessor to terminfo, created by Bill Joy in 1978 for vi. Text-based format with 2-character capability codes. Largely replaced by terminfo but still referenced in documentation.

ncurses.description: Free reimplementation of the Unix curses TUI library, maintained by Thomas Dickey since 1996. The most widely used TUI library on Unix systems. Uses terminfo for terminal capability lookup.

wcwidth.description: A C function that returns the display width (1 or 2 columns) of a Unicode character. Based on Unicode's East Asian Width property (UAX #11). Predates emoji and doesn't handle grapheme clusters, variation selectors, or ZWJ sequences.

ZWJ.description: A Unicode character (U+200D) that joins adjacent characters into a single grapheme cluster. Used in emoji sequences: 👨‍👩‍👧 is three emoji joined by two ZWJs. Terminals must handle these for correct cursor positioning.

VS16.description: A Unicode character (U+FE0F) that forces emoji presentation of the preceding character. Without VS16, some characters display as text (1 column); with VS16, they display as emoji (2 columns). Terminal width calculation must account for this.

C1.description: Single-byte control characters in the 0x80–0x9F range defined by ECMA-48. Include CSI (0x9B), OSC (0x9D), DCS (0x90). Rarely used in practice because they conflict with UTF-8 encoding — the 7-bit ESC-prefixed forms are universal instead.

BEL.description: ASCII character 0x07. Originally rang a physical bell. In modern terminals, may flash the title bar, play a sound, or trigger a notification. Also used as the string terminator in some OSC sequences (alternative to ST).

ST.description: The ESC \ sequence that ends OSC, DCS, and other string-type escape sequences. BEL (0x07) is accepted as an alternative terminator by most terminals.

modifyOtherKeys.description: An xterm mode that reports modifier keys for non-special characters. Predates the Kitty keyboard protocol. Enabled via CSI > 4 ; 2 m. Less comprehensive than Kitty's protocol but more widely supported in older terminals.

CSI u.description: A keyboard reporting format proposed by Leonerd (Paul Evans) in the fixterms specification. Uses CSI ... u sequences for unambiguous key reporting. Influenced the Kitty keyboard protocol, which extended and formalized the approach.

Sixel.description: A raster image format from DEC (1983) that encodes images as printable ASCII characters, each representing a 1×6 pixel column. Revived in modern terminals for inline image display. Uses DCS sequences.

OSC 52.description: Escape sequence for reading/writing the system clipboard. Allows terminal applications to access the clipboard over SSH without X11 forwarding. Some terminals disable read access for security.

OSC 8.description: Escape sequence for clickable hyperlinks in terminal output. Text between OSC 8 start and end markers becomes a link. Supported by most modern terminals.

CHA.description: Move cursor to column N on the current row.

CNL.description: Move cursor to the beginning of the line N rows down.

IL.description: Insert N blank lines at cursor position, pushing existing lines down.

DL.description: Delete N lines at cursor position, pulling lines up from below.

SU.description: Scroll the display up N lines. New blank lines appear at the bottom.

SD.description: Scroll the display down N lines. New blank lines appear at the top.

ECH.description: Erase N characters at cursor position without moving the cursor.

Kitty keyboard protocol.description: Unambiguous key reporting with modifiers and release events. Solves Ctrl+I/Tab ambiguity. Adopted by Ghostty, WezTerm, foot.

Kitty graphics protocol.description: Inline image display via chunked base64 transfer. More capable than Sixel but less widely supported.

bracketed paste mode.description: Terminal wraps pasted text in markers so the shell knows it is pasted, not typed. Prevents accidental command execution.

alternate screen buffer.description: A separate screen for full-screen apps. When the app exits, previous content reappears.

focus reporting.description: Terminal notifies the app when the window gains or loses focus. Enables apps to pause/resume rendering.

scroll region.description: Define a range of lines that scroll independently. Used by TUIs for status bars, headers.

TTY.description: Originally a physical printing terminal. Now refers to the kernel subsystem that manages terminal I/O — the TTY driver, line discipline, and pseudo-terminal (PTY) devices.

raw mode.description: A TTY line discipline setting where every byte is delivered to the application immediately — no echo, no line editing, no signal generation. What TUI apps use. Opposite of canonical mode.

canonical mode.description: The default TTY line discipline mode where input is line-buffered (delivered on Enter), with echo and basic line editing (backspace, Ctrl+U). Also called cooked mode.

line discipline.description: A kernel-level module that sits between the PTY and the application. Handles echo, line editing, signal generation (Ctrl+C → SIGINT), and newline translation. Controlled via stty.

process group.description: A set of processes that receive signals together. The foreground process group gets keyboard signals (SIGINT, SIGTSTP). Background groups are stopped if they try to read from the terminal.

job control.description: Shell feature for managing foreground/background processes. Ctrl+Z suspends (SIGTSTP), fg resumes in foreground, bg resumes in background. Relies on the kernel's process group and TTY subsystem.

C0.description: The 33 non-printable ASCII characters in the 0x00–0x1F range plus DEL (0x7F). Includes ESC, BS, TAB, LF, CR, and BEL. Predates escape sequences — these are single-byte terminal commands.

OSC 133.description: Invisible markers for shell prompt, command, and output boundaries. Enables click-to-navigate between commands.

OSC 633.description: VS Code extension of OSC 133 with additional markers for command detection and shell state.

OSC 7.description: Report the shell current directory to the terminal. Used for tab titles and new-tab-in-same-directory.

OSC 0.description: Set the terminal window title and icon name.

OSC 4.description: Query or set entries in the terminal 256-color palette.

OSC 10.description: Query the terminal default foreground color. Response is an X11 color string.

OSC 11.description: Query the terminal default background color. Used for light/dark mode detection.

OSC 1337.description: iTerm2 proprietary extensions including inline images, shell integration, and capability reporting.

SGR 38.description: Extended foreground color: SGR 38;5;N for 256-color, SGR 38;2;R;G;B for truecolor.

SGR 48.description: Extended background color: SGR 48;5;N for 256-color, SGR 48;2;R;G;B for truecolor.

Mode 2004.description: Terminal wraps pasted text in markers so the shell knows it is pasted, not typed.

Mode 2026.description: Buffer all output between start/end markers and paint as one frame. Eliminates flicker.

Mode 1049.description: Switch to alternate screen buffer and save/restore cursor position.

Unicode baseline.description: Unicode correctness — wide chars, combining, emoji

Kitty.description: GPU-accelerated terminal by Kovid Goyal. Pioneer of the Kitty keyboard and graphics protocols.

Ghostty.description: GPU-accelerated terminal by Mitchell Hashimoto, written in Zig with excellent standards compliance.

WezTerm.description: Rust terminal emulator and multiplexer by Wez Furlong with Lua configuration and SSH domain support.

Alacritty.description: GPU-accelerated minimal terminal written in Rust. Pioneered GPU rendering for terminals in 2017.

xterm.js.description: The most widely deployed terminal emulator — powers VS Code, Cursor, and countless web terminals.

vterm.js.description: Full-featured TypeScript terminal emulator targeting 100% of the terminfo.dev feature matrix.

vt100.js.description: Lightweight pure TypeScript VT100/VT220 terminal emulator with zero dependencies.

tmux.description: The most widely used terminal multiplexer — session persistence, window management, and pane splitting.

GNU Screen.description: The original terminal multiplexer (1987). Session persistence and detach/reattach for remote work.

UTF-8.description: Variable-length encoding (1-4 bytes) that maps every Unicode code point to a byte sequence. Backward-compatible with ASCII. The universal encoding for modern terminals, designed by Rob Pike and Ken Thompson in 1992.

UTF-16.description: Variable-length encoding using 2 or 4 bytes per code point. Used internally by JavaScript and Windows. Not used in terminal I/O — terminals use UTF-8 exclusively.

UTF-32.description: Fixed-width encoding using 4 bytes per code point. Simple indexing but wasteful. Used internally by some text processing libraries but never for terminal wire format.

UCS-2.description: Fixed 2-byte encoding covering the Basic Multilingual Plane (U+0000-U+FFFF). Predecessor to UTF-16. Cannot represent emoji or supplementary characters.

ASCII.expansion: American Standard Code for Information Interchange
ASCII.description: 7-bit character encoding (128 characters) published in 1963. The first 32 codes are control characters (C0), the rest are printable. UTF-8 is a superset — all ASCII text is valid UTF-8.

ISO 8859-1.description: 8-bit extension of ASCII adding 96 Western European characters (accented letters, symbols). Was the default encoding before UTF-8 took over. The first 256 Unicode code points match Latin-1 exactly.

code point.description: A number assigned to a character in the Unicode standard, written as U+XXXX (e.g. U+0041 is 'A'). Unicode defines over 149,000 code points. A single visible character (grapheme cluster) may consist of multiple code points.

grapheme.description: The smallest unit of a writing system that a user perceives as a single character. In Unicode, a grapheme cluster may span multiple code points — an accented letter, a flag emoji, or a family emoji are each one grapheme.

BOM.description: The Unicode character U+FEFF placed at the start of a file to signal encoding and byte order. Useful for UTF-16/UTF-32 but unnecessary and discouraged for UTF-8. Can cause problems when piped to terminals.

control character.description: Non-printable characters in the 0x00-0x1F (C0) and 0x80-0x9F (C1) ranges. Includes ESC (0x1B), BEL (0x07), BS (0x08), TAB (0x09), LF (0x0A), and CR (0x0D). Terminals interpret these as commands rather than displayable text.

LF.description: ASCII character 0x0A. Moves the cursor down one line. In terminals, the line discipline typically translates LF to CR+LF on output (the onlcr setting). Unix convention uses LF alone as the line ending.

CR.description: ASCII character 0x0D. Moves the cursor to the beginning of the current line without advancing to the next line. Named after the typewriter carriage that physically returned to the left margin.

CRLF.description: The two-byte sequence CR (0x0D) followed by LF (0x0A). The standard line ending for terminal protocols and Windows text files. Terminals expect CRLF — the line discipline converts bare LF to CRLF on output.

BS.description: ASCII character 0x08. Moves the cursor one column left without erasing. In canonical mode the line discipline handles destructive backspace (BS + Space + BS). In raw mode the application must handle it.

TAB.description: ASCII character 0x09. Advances the cursor to the next tab stop (default every 8 columns). Tab stops can be set and cleared with the HTS and TBC sequences.

ESC.description: ASCII character 0x1B. The byte that begins all escape sequences. Followed by [ for CSI, ] for OSC, P for DCS, or other characters for simpler sequences. Named because it 'escapes' from normal text interpretation.

DEL.description: ASCII character 0x7F. Originally punched all 7 holes in paper tape to 'delete' a character. In modern terminals, often sent by the Backspace key. Not part of the C0 range despite being a control character.

stty.description: Unix command to configure the TTY line discipline: echo, canonical mode, signal characters (Ctrl+C, Ctrl+Z), baud rate, and newline translation. TUI apps use stty (or tcsetattr) to enter raw mode.

echo.description: The line discipline's automatic display of typed characters. In canonical mode, the kernel echoes input. In raw mode, echo is disabled and the application decides what to display. Controlled via stty echo/stty -echo.

baud rate.description: The data transmission speed of a serial connection, measured in symbols per second. Physical terminals ran at 300-19200 baud. Modern pseudo-terminals ignore baud rate but the field still exists in the termios struct.

terminal multiplexer.description: Software that lets you run multiple terminal sessions inside one connection, with session persistence across disconnects. tmux and GNU Screen are terminal multiplexers. They must parse and re-emit escape sequences, which can cause feature loss.

cursor.description: The blinking indicator showing where the next character will appear. Terminals support block, underline, and bar shapes (DECSCUSR), can show/hide it (DECTCEM), and report its position (DSR 6). TUI apps rely on precise cursor control.

cell.description: One character position in the terminal grid. Each cell holds a character, foreground/background colors, and attributes (bold, italic, etc.). Wide characters occupy two cells. The terminal screen is a grid of rows x columns of cells.

glyph.description: The visual representation of a character as rendered by a font. A single code point may have multiple glyphs (ligatures, contextual forms), and multiple code points may produce one glyph (grapheme clusters).

ligature.description: Two or more characters rendered as a single combined glyph. Programming ligatures (-> becomes an arrow, != becomes not-equal) are popular in coding fonts like Fira Code and JetBrains Mono. Not all terminals support ligature rendering.

monospace.description: A font where every character occupies the same horizontal width. Essential for terminal rendering since the character grid assumes uniform cell widths. Wide (CJK/emoji) characters occupy exactly two cells.

font fallback.description: When a character is missing from the primary font, the terminal searches a chain of fallback fonts for a glyph. Critical for emoji and CJK rendering. Poor fallback causes tofu (missing glyph rectangles).

ECMA-35.description: Standard for coded character set structure and extension techniques. Defines the G0-G3 character set designation and invocation mechanism used by VT terminals. Largely obsolete — replaced by UTF-8.

ISO 6429.description: The ISO version of ECMA-48 — the foundational terminal control sequence standard. Published alongside ANSI X3.64 (now withdrawn). When someone says 'ANSI escape codes' they mean ISO 6429 / ECMA-48 sequences.

POSIX.description: IEEE standard defining the Unix API, including terminal I/O (termios), job control, and process groups. The termios API (tcgetattr/tcsetattr) is how programs configure raw mode, echo, and signal handling.

DA2.description: Query for the terminal type, firmware version, and hardware options. Sent as CSI > c. Unlike DA1 (capability flags), DA2 identifies the specific terminal product and version.

DECRQM.description: Query whether a specific DEC private mode is currently set, reset, or unrecognized. The terminal responds with DECRPM. Essential for feature detection without side effects.

SGR mouse.description: Extended mouse reporting format using CSI < sequences with semicolon-delimited parameters. Supports coordinates beyond column 223 (the limit of X10 encoding). The modern standard for mouse tracking in terminals.

iTerm2 inline images.description: Protocol for displaying images inline in terminal output using base64-encoded data in OSC 1337 sequences. Adopted by several terminals beyond iTerm2 including WezTerm and mintty.

focus events.description: Terminal sends escape sequences when the window gains or loses keyboard focus. Lets TUI apps pause rendering when not visible and refresh when refocused.

DECRC.description: Restore cursor position and attributes previously saved with DECSC. Together DECSC/DECRC are used by TUI apps to preserve cursor state across screen updates.

SPA.description: ECMA-48 sequence marking the beginning of a protected area that cannot be erased by selective erase operations. Rarely used in modern terminals.

SM.description: CSI Pm h — enable an ECMA-48 standard mode (insert mode, auto-wrap). Distinct from DECSET (CSI ? Pm h) which controls DEC private modes.

RM.description: CSI Pm l — disable an ECMA-48 standard mode. The counterpart to SM. Distinct from DECRST (CSI ? Pm l) for DEC private modes.

IRM.description: ECMA-48 mode 4. When set (SM 4), typed characters push existing text right instead of overwriting. When reset (RM 4), characters overwrite. Most terminals default to replace mode.

HTS.description: Set a tab stop at the current cursor column. Used with TBC (tab clear) to customize tab stop positions beyond the default every-8-columns.

TBC.description: Clear tab stops. TBC 0 clears the stop at the current column; TBC 3 clears all tab stops.

DECALN.description: Fill the entire screen with the letter 'E'. Originally used to test CRT screen alignment. Now useful for debugging — it instantly shows the screen dimensions and clears all content.

REP.description: CSI Pn b — repeat the last printed character N times. A bandwidth optimization that avoids sending the same character repeatedly. Supported by most modern terminals.

VPA.description: CSI Pn d — move cursor to row N on the current column. The vertical counterpart to CHA (horizontal absolute).

CPL.description: Move cursor to the beginning of the line N rows up. The counterpart to CNL (Cursor Next Line).

DECSCA.description: Mark characters as protected or unprotected. Protected characters are not affected by selective erase operations (DECSED, DECSEL). Rarely used.

Unicode.description: Universal character encoding standard covering over 149,000 characters from all modern writing systems plus emoji, symbols, and historic scripts. Terminal correctness requires handling width (wcwidth), grapheme clusters, and combining characters.

combining character.description: A Unicode character that attaches to the preceding base character without advancing the cursor. Diacritical marks (accents, umlauts) and some emoji modifiers are combining characters. Terminals must render them in the same cell as the base character.

tofu.description: The small rectangle displayed when a font lacks a glyph for a character. Named for its resemblance to a block of tofu. Common with emoji and rare scripts when font fallback fails.

charset designation.description: The ECMA-35/VT mechanism for switching between character sets using escape sequences. G0-G3 are four slots that can be loaded with different charsets (ASCII, DEC Special Graphics, etc.). Largely obsolete — replaced by UTF-8.

DEC Special Graphics.description: A character set that maps ASCII printable characters to line-drawing symbols (box corners, horizontal/vertical lines, etc.). Activated via ESC ( 0. Used by ncurses for borders. Predates Unicode box-drawing characters.

DECSCNM.description: DEC private mode ?5 — swap foreground and background colors for the entire screen. Used for light-on-dark vs dark-on-light display.

DECCOLM.description: DEC private mode ?3 — switch between 80 and 132 column mode. Originally for wide printouts on DEC terminals. Modern terminals typically resize the window instead.

DECOM.description: DEC private mode ?6 — when set, cursor positions are relative to the scroll region instead of the full screen. Affects CUP and other absolute positioning commands.

XTVERSION.description: Query the terminal for its name and version string. Sent as CSI > 0 q (or DCS in some terminals). Response format varies by terminal. Useful for terminal detection.

XTWINOPS.description: CSI t sequences for window manipulation — resize, move, minimize, report size in pixels or characters. Some operations are disabled by default for security.

soft reset.description: CSI ! p — partial terminal reset. Resets modes, character sets, and cursor state without clearing the screen or scrollback. Less disruptive than RIS (full reset).

primary DA.description: The most fundamental terminal query — CSI c. The terminal responds with capability flags indicating what features it supports. Also used for terminal detection since different terminals report different values.
